coredata.dc:title: Intrinsic pentagon defect engineering in multiple spatial-scale carbon frameworks for efficient triiodide reduction

coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Siyi
Song, Xuedan
Yu, Chang
Chang, Jiangwei
Ding, Yiwang
Liu, Yingbin
Zhang, Xiubo
Liu, Weizhe
Qiu, Jieshan
coredata.dc:description: Intrinsic topological defect engineering has been proven as a promising strategy to elevate the electrocatalytic activity of carbon materials. However, the controllable construction of high-density and specific topological defects in carbon frameworks to reveal the relationship between reactivity and defect structure remains a challenging task. Herein, the intrinsic pentagon carbon sites that can favor electron overflow and enhance their binding affinity towards the intermediates of catalytic reaction are firstly presented by the work function and the p-band center calculations. To experimentally verify this, the cage-opening reaction of fullerene is proposed and utilized for synthesizing carbon quantum dots with specific pentagon configuration (CQDs-P), subsequently utilizing CQDs-P to modulate the micro-scale defect density of three-dimensional reduced graphene oxide (rGO) via π-π interactions. The multiple spatial-scale rGO-conjugated CQDs-P structure simultaneously possesses abundant pentagon and edge defects as catalytic active sites and long-range-ordered π electron delocalization system as conductive network. The defects-rich CQDs-P/rGO-4 all-carbon-based catalyst exhibits superb catalytic activity for triiodide reduction reaction with a high photoelectric conversion efficiency of 8.40%, superior to the Pt reference (7.97%). Theoretical calculations suggest that pentagon defects in the carbon frameworks can promote charge transfer and modulate the adsorption/dissociation behavior of the reaction intermediates, thus enhancing the electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst. This work confirms the role of intrinsic pentagon defects in catalytic reactions and provides a new insight into the synthesis of defects-rich carbon catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Defect engineering
Pentagon carbon
Carbon quantum dots
Electrocatalytic activity
Triiodide reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Work function and (b) partial density of states (PDOS) of C6, C5-b, and C5-e structure models.

(a) Illustration of the synthesis process of CQDs-P. (b) MALDI-TOF-MS spectra of CQDs-P and C60. (c) XRD patterns of CQDs-P and C60. (d) TEM image of CQDs-P (inset: the lateral size distribution). (e) HRTEM image and (f) the corresponding SAED pattern of CQDs-P. (g) Raman spectra of CQDs-P and C60 (inset: magnified Raman spectrum of CQDs-P). (h) EPR spectra of CQDs-P and C60.

CIE (1931) chromaticity diagrams of (a) CQDs-P and (c) r-CQDs-P (inset: the digital images of CQDs-P and r-CQDs-P aqueous dispersion under 254 nm UV light). Three-dimensional excitation-emission photoluminescence spectra of (b) CQDs-P and (d) r-CQDs-P. (e) Tauc plots of CQDs-P and r-CQDs-P. (f) Schematic of photoluminescence mechanism of CQDs-P and r-CQDs-P.

SEM images of (a) rGO and (b) CQDs-P/rGO-4. (c) TEM image of CQDs-P/rGO-4. (d) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, (e) XRD patterns, and (f) XPS survey spectra of GO, rGO, and CQDs-P/rGO-4. (g) Raman spectra of rGO and CQDs-P/rGO-x.

(a) J-V curves of DSSCs based on rGO, CQDs-P/rGO-x, and Pt CEs (inset: the magnified curves of the black dashed box). (b) Relationship between PCE and J Ared. (c) CV curves of rGO and CQDs-P/rGO-x measured in [Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4- redox couple at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (inset: the calculated values of S e and the detailed calculation method). (d) Relationship between S e and I D:I G. (e) Electrochemical stability of the symmetrical dummy cells made of CQDs-P/rGO-4 and Pt.

(a) Charge density difference and Bader charge analysis for various adsorption configurations. The yellow and cyan iso-surfaces stand for charge accumulation and depletion, respectively, the iso-surface value is 0.00015 e Å−3. (b) Adsorption energy of I2 and the distance of I–C on various adsorption sites. (c) The bond length of I–I on various adsorption sites. (d) Catalytic mechanism of pentagon defects in all-carbon-based catalysts for IRR.

head.ce:title: Intrinsic pentagon defect engineering in multiple spatial-scale carbon frameworks for efficient triiodide reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Frontier Science Center for Smart Materials

Frontier Science Center for Smart Materials

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pentagon-rich carbon quantum dots were constructed and subsequently regulated the micro-scale defect density of three-dimensional reduced graphene oxide, which facilitates charge transfer and modulates the adsorption/dissociation behavior of reaction intermediates, accounting for its excellent performance.

Intrinsic topological defect engineering has been proven as a promising strategy to elevate the electrocatalytic activity of carbon materials. However, the controllable construction of high-density and specific topological defects in carbon frameworks to reveal the relationship between reactivity and defect structure remains a challenging task. Herein, the intrinsic pentagon carbon sites that can favor electron overflow and enhance their binding affinity towards the intermediates of catalytic reaction are firstly presented by the work function and the p-band center calculations. To experimentally verify this, the cage-opening reaction of fullerene is proposed and utilized for synthesizing carbon quantum dots with specific pentagon configuration (CQDs-P), subsequently utilizing CQDs-P to modulate the micro-scale defect density of three-dimensional reduced graphene oxide (rGO) via π-π interactions. The multiple spatial-scale rGO-conjugated CQDs-P structure simultaneously possesses abundant pentagon and edge defects as catalytic active sites and long-range-ordered π electron delocalization system as conductive network. The defects-rich CQDs-P/rGO-4 all-carbon-based catalyst exhibits superb catalytic activity for triiodide reduction reaction with a high photoelectric conversion efficiency of 8.40%, superior to the Pt reference (7.97%). Theoretical calculations suggest that pentagon defects in the carbon frameworks can promote charge transfer and modulate the adsorption/dissociation behavior of the reaction intermediates, thus enhancing the electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst. This work confirms the role of intrinsic pentagon defects in catalytic reactions and provides a new insight into the synthesis of defects-rich carbon catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Defect engineering has been identified as a predominant strategy to elevate the inherently electrocatalytic activity of carbon for energy applications, especially the atomic-level topological defects and edge defects [1–5]. The intentionally introduced defect sites in the carbon frameworks can disturb the electronic symmetry of the aromatic rings and increase the potentially catalytic active sites, thus boosting the catalytic performance of the carbon-based catalysts, rendering them high reactivity comparable to commercial precious metal catalysts [6–8]. According to the Second Law of Thermodynamics, the substances/materials tend to entropy increase, accompanied by the increase of disorder and chaos features during the transformation process to some extent [9,10]. As such, it is inevitable to introduce intrinsic defects in materials due to limited fabrication techniques, and these defects are key determinants of electrochemical activity. However, the micro-scale defect density is too low to generate high electrocatalytic performance at the macroscopic level [3]. Hence, the critical challenge remains in constructing the high-density intrinsic defects in carbon frameworks [11].

Besides the most common and well-known edge defects, topological defects (pentagons, heptagons, or octagons in the carbon skeleton as non-hexagonal structures), and in particular the topological pentagon defects are commonly recognized as active centers of electrocatalysis because they can locally induce Gaussian curvature, alter bond lengths and angles, and possess rehybridized electron orbitals at the atomic level [12]. These properties confer local charge/spin redistribution at the topological sites, leading to enhanced electrocatalytic activity [11]. Topological pentagon defects can be constructed by rotating or rearranging the newly generated carbon dangling bonds [3]. Unfortunately, the high formation energy of defects in the pristine carbon network determines the significant difficulties in removing the lattice carbon atoms of the six-membered ring. Typically, topological pentagon defects are generated by first doping highly electronegative nitrogen atoms into the carbon frameworks to break the electrical neutrality of the matrix, then removing the highly reactive nitrogen atoms from the nitrogen-doped carbon materials by high-temperature thermal treatments to generate the various defect regions (such as pentagon-octagon-pentagon, heptagon-pentagon-pentagon-heptagon) [5,13]. It is quite difficult to controllably synthesize structure-specific defects. Accordingly, the construction of specific structure-containing topological defects with high density and the identification of intrinsic reactivity are indispensable for unlocking the relationship between reactivity and defect structure, which will comprehensively improve the versatility of carbon and is a pivotal issue for designing efficient carbon-based catalysts [14].

Herein, to confirm the positive effect of pentagon defects on the electrocatalytic activity of carbon materials, the electronic structures were first investigated by calculating the work function and the p-band center. The results suggest that the intrinsic pentagon carbon sites indeed favor electron overflow and enhance their binding ability towards the intermediates of catalytic reaction compared to the common hexagon ones. Under the guidance of theoretical investigation, the chemical cage-opening reaction of fullerene (C60) is adopted to elaborately construct topological pentagon defects-structured carbon quantum dots (CQDs-P) made of carbon clusters, which possess an abundance of inherited pentagon defects and introduced edge defects, with a higher degree of defects than that of the precursor C60. The resultant CQDs-P with specific defect sites are utilized to regulate and increase the micro-scale defect density within the three-dimensional (3D) reduced graphene oxide (rGO) frameworks via π-π interactions to fabricate CQDs-P-conjugated-rGO (CQDs-P/rGO) all-carbon catalyst. This multiple spatial-scale structure with enriched active centers and excellent conductive framework significantly improves the electrocatalytic performance of the catalyst. Using I3 − reduction as a probe reaction, the dye-sensitized solar cell assembled by CQDs-P/rGO-4 exhibits an impressive photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) of 8.40%, outperforming the commercial Pt (7.97%). Theoretical calculations reveal that intrinsic pentagon defects in carbon networks induce Gaussian curvature and promote charge transfer at pentagon sites, thus favoring the adsorption of electrophilic I2 molecule (intermediate of I3 − reaction), accounting for its excellent performance. This work unravels the intrinsic contributions of specific pentagon defects on carbon at the nano/micro-scaled level to catalytic activity at the macroscopic level, illustrating the immense potential of defective carbons toward energy conversion.

Theoretical calculations based on DFT were employed to confirm the effect of pentagon defects in carbon materials on the electrochemical performance. The two typical models involved topological pentagon defects were constructed by the graphene with a pentagon ring on the basal plane (C5-b) and edge (C5-e) sites. Furthermore, the pristine graphene model (C6) was also constructed for comparison. The work function and the p-band center were calculated to investigate the electronic structures of catalysts. As observed from Fig. 1 (a), the work function values of the C5-b (4.58 eV) and C5-e (4.46 eV) are smaller than that of C6 (4.69 eV), which indicates that electrons are more likely to overflow from the C5 surface, thus promoting the electrocatalytic reaction [15]. Meanwhile, the p-band center values (Fig. 1b) of pentagon defects (−4.57 eV in C5-b and −4.48 eV in C5-e) are closer to the Fermi energy level compared to hexagons (−4.64 eV in C6). This result suggests that the pentagon carbon sites in carbon materials can serve as potentially active sites to enhance the binding ability towards the intermediates of catalytic reaction and facilitate the dissociation of intermediate molecules compared to hexagon ones [16]. The contribution of nano-scale pentagon defects to the electrocatalytic performance of carbon materials at the macroscopic level will be demonstrated by experiments.

To experimentally verify the effects of intrinsic pentagons on electrochemical reactivity, the chemical oxidation method was utilized to rupture C60 molecules (pentagon-rich precursors with twelve pentagons and twenty hexagons) into small carbon clusters made of pentagon defects. Subsequently, these carbon clusters with high surface energy were diffused and assembled to form the CQDs-P (Fig. 2 a). Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization-time of flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF-MS) (Fig. 2b) suggests that the as-prepared CQDs-P exist as small carbon fragments without the characteristic peaks of C60 (m/z = 720), demonstrating the efficient cage-opening of C60. Apart from that, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 2c) also confirm the successful cage-opening of C60. Obviously, CQDs-P merely show a broad diffraction peak, while the thirteen characteristic peaks of C60 are not observed, indicative of an amorphous structure of CQDs-P [17,18]. The morphology of CQDs-P was observed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The CQDs-P exhibit quasi-spherical particles with amorphous character and well dispersion (Fig. 2d–2f). The lateral sizes are distributed in the range of 1.0 to 6.4 nm. The ultrasmall-sized CQDs-P with surface effects provide a preferable capability that can anchor intimately on the rGO during the assembly process.

Raman spectra were presented to evidence whether the CQDs-P structure inherits the intrinsic pentagon defects of C60. As shown in Fig. 2(g), the breathing mode [Ag(1)] and radial modes [Hg(1)-Hg(4)] located at lower wavenumbers disappear with the transition from C60 to CQDs-P, whereas the typical vibration modes related to pentagon pinch [Ag(2)], pentagon shear [Hg(7)], and hexagon shear [Hg(8)] are still present [19,20]. Meanwhile, a new vibrational band at 1340 cm−1 correlates with the disorder-induced D band, reflecting the presence of defects (missing atoms in the lattice, bound species, etc.) on CQDs-P [21–23]. The defect degree of CQDs-P and C60 was further evaluated by electron-paramagnetic resonance (EPR) (Fig. 2h). The significantly enhanced intensity at g = 2.003 in the EPR spectra indicates that more unpaired electrons exist in CQDs-P than in C60, which would be the presence of numerous pentagon and edge defects [24]. These variations manifest that the C60 cage has been broken into fragments by chemical oxidation, and the intrinsic pentagon defects are inherited in CQDs-P.

Subsequently, the chemical structure changes of CQDs-P and C60 were characterized by Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Fig. S1). Abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., epoxy, hydroxyl, and carboxyl) are introduced on the surface of CQDs-P during chemical oxidation, accompanied by the disappearance of the characteristic vibration modes for C60 (F1u), which also substantiates the disruption of the C60 cage [25,26]. More quantitative information on the chemical composition of CQDs-P was detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The atomic ratio of oxygen is 34.40% for CQDs-P (Table S1), implying highly oxidized state of their surface. The C 1s peaks (Fig. S2a) are composed of five components located at 284.6, 285.6, 286.8, 288.0, and 288.9 eV, which are identified as C=C, C–C, C–O, C=O, and O–C=O bonds, respectively [27,28]. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2b) can be deconvoluted into C=O (531.6 eV), O–C=O (532.5 eV), and C–OH (533.2 eV) bonds, which facilitate the well dispersion of CQDs-P in water [29].

The remarkable optical characteristics of CQDs-P were examined by Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy to further corroborate the formed CQDs-P. The aqueous dispersion of CQDs-P exhibits green emission with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.32, 0.39) (Fig. 3 a). Two absorption bands are observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S3) at 240 and 283 nm, which are attributed to π-π* (C=C) and n-π* (C=O) electronic transitions in CQDs-P [30]. CQDs-P (Fig. S3 and Fig. 3b) with the maximum emission and excitation wavelength at ca. 535 nm and ca. 400 nm exhibit a typical excitation-dependent PL behavior, probably induced by the different emissive sites on the surface of CQDs-P [31]. Hence, the PL of CQDs-P may originate from the surface-state emission associated with surface defects (e.g., oxygen-containing functional groups), which introduce various emission sites and cause variations in the emission spectra [32]. To further substantiate the successful fabrication of CQDs-P and to elucidate the mechanism of surface-state PL, NaBH4 was utilized as a reductant to generate reduced CQDs-P (r-CQDs-P). Intriguingly, r-CQDs-P exhibit bright blue emission under 254 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 3c), and the PL intensity is higher than that of CQDs-P according to the digital photos and PL spectra. UV-vis absorption and PL spectra of r-CQDs-P (Fig. S4 and Fig. 3d) show a blue shift and a similar excitation-dependent PL behavior. FTIR and XPS analyses were carried out to investigate variations in oxidation degree and surface functional groups. The atomic ratio of oxygen (Table S1) decreases from 34.40% in CQDs-P to 29.73% in r-CQDs-P. The lower oxidation degree of r-CQDs-P leads to fewer surface defects, resulting in blue-shifted emission and higher energy level (Fig. 3e) [33]. Furthermore, the decrement of the electron-attracting groups (C=O) and the increment of the electron-donating groups (C-OH) (Figs. S5 and S6, Fig. 3f) are responsible for the increased intensity of PL [34]. Taken together, CQDs-P with pentagon defects were successfully prepared by cage-opening reaction of C60.

The CQDs-P with good monodispersity as pentagon-rich carbon nanoparticles regulate the defect density of rGO to increase the catalytic active sites. In a typical experiment, the aqueous dispersion of CQDs-P was mixed with graphene oxide (GO) in a specific ratio. The subsequent hydrothermal treatment transformed the GO nanosheets into rGO nanosheets and self-assembled into 3D graphene hydrogels via π-π interactions. Finally, the CQDs-P/rGO-x (x refers to the mass of CQDs-P) hydrogels were freeze-dried and annealed to obtain CQDs-P/rGO-x all-carbon-based catalysts with multiple spatial-scale structure.

For simplification, CQDs-P/rGO-4 was used as a representative sample to investigate the macro-scale morphology and micro-scale pore structure characteristics. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 4 a and b, Fig. S7) reveal that rGO and CQDs-P/rGO-4 form a unique 3D spatial-scale framework upon hydrothermal reaction, compared to the sheet-like structure of GO. TEM images of rGO (Fig. S8) and CQDs-P/rGO-4 (Fig. 4c) confirm that rGO presents a porous structure, and CQDs-P are successfully anchored on the surface of rGO. The N2 physisorption measurements further characterized the pore structure parameters of GO, rGO, and CQDs-P/rGO-4. Their N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. 4d) exhibit typical IV-type hysteresis loop, indicating the existence of mesopore. The specific surface areas (SSA) of rGO (363 m2 g−1) and CQDs-P/rGO-4 (554 m2 g−1) are much larger than that of GO (16 m2 g−1). This result suggests that the configuration of multiple spatial-scale structure contributes to increase the SSA of the carbon materials, which promotes the contact of electrolyte with the catalyst and exposes more catalytic active sites. The pore size distribution curve (Fig. S9) of CQDs-P/rGO-4 reveals a hierarchical porous structure, favoring ion transport kinetics.

To investigate the compositions and chemical states of the samples, XRD, XPS, and FTIR were performed, respectively. It can be seen from the XRD patterns (Fig. 4e) that the appearance of graphite (002) diffraction peak in rGO is accompanied by the disappearance of graphite (001) diffraction peak during hydrothermal process, suggesting the recovery of the surface π-conjugated system, which facilitates the conjugation with CQDs-P [35]. Compared with rGO, the full width at half-maximum of CQDs-P/rGO-x (Fig. 4e and Fig. S10) gradually increases with increasing content of CQDs-P, implying the increased defect degree. FTIR and XPS analyses (Figs. S11 and S12, Fig. 4f) illustrate that the oxygen content of rGO decreases drastically during the reduction process, but increases slightly by coupling CQDs-P, which further confirms the successful fabrication of rGO-conjugated CQDs-P. Furthermore, these results also signify that no new chemical bonds are formed in CQDs-P/rGO compared to rGO, implying their coupling by non-covalent interactions. For further unraveling the interaction modes between CQDs-P and rGO, UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were measured (Fig. S13). CQDs-P/rGO-4 shows a red-shifted absorption peak in comparison with rGO, suggesting π-π interactions between them [36]. In PL emission spectra (Fig. S14), the fluorescence emission of CQDs-P is almost completely quenched upon integration on rGO, reaffirming the existence of π-π interactions [37].

The controllable regulation of the micro-scale defect density within rGO by CQDs-P was characterized by Raman spectroscopy. In Fig. 4(g) and Fig. S15, all samples display the well-defined D-band (caused by sp 3 defects) and G-band (induced by sp 2-hybrid carbon) at 1340 and 1589 cm−1. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (I D/I G) is used to assess the defect density in carbon materials [38]. Notably, the I D/I G value of rGO (1.27) is significantly higher than that of GO (0.95), which is attributed to the 3D spatial-scale porous structure of rGO exposing more edge catalytic active sites. For CQDs-P/rGO-x, the I D/I G value increases from 1.29 (CQDs-P/rGO-1) to 1.42 (CQDs-P/rGO-4) with increased CQDs-P, due to the presence of numerous intrinsic defects (pentagon defects and edge defects) in CQDs-P. However, the I D/I G value of CQDs-P/rGO-5 decreases to 1.40 with further increased CQDs-P, probably owing to the aggregation of excess CQDs-P. To prove the above speculation, CQDs-P/rGO-5 was characterized by TEM and N2 physisorption. As can be seen from the results (Figs. S16 and S17), the partial CQDs-P agglomerate into tens of nanometers, and the SSA of CQDs-P/rGO-5 is reduced to 447 m2 g−1 (554 m2 g−1 in CQDs-P/rGO-4). Therefore, the monodisperse feature of CQDs-P on the surface of rGO is essential to increase the intrinsic defects, which serve as potential active centers.

The effect of intrinsic defect density of CQDs-P/rGO-x on catalytic activity was investigated using the triiodide reduction reaction (IRR) as a probe. The photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves (Fig. 5 a) were evaluated, and the detailed photovoltaic parameters were summarized in Table S2. Expectedly, the photovoltaic performance of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) with CQDs-P/rGO-x counter electrodes (CEs) is significantly better than that of the pristine rGO and enhanced with the increased defect density. Thereinto, the CQDs-P/rGO-4 catalyst with the highest defect density exhibits excellent photovoltaic performance with a PCE of 8.40%, superior to commercial Pt (7.97%). Compared with reported heteroatom-doped graphene or graphene composites (Fig. S18), the all-carbon-based CQDs-P/rGO-4 catalyst displays higher or comparable PCE. These results suggest that the intrinsic pentagon defects in carbon materials exhibit excellent catalytic activity and can be used as effective catalytic active sites for electrochemical reactions.

For investigating the potential reason for the improved performance of the intrinsic pentagon defects, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed to further assess the catalytic behavior of all samples toward IRR. In Fig. S19, a pair of characteristic A ox/A red peaks is observed, corresponding to the redox reaction of the I3 −/I− redox couple. The CE of DSSC is liable for catalyzing the reduction of I3 − to I− (I3 − + 2e− → 3 I−), thus the peak current density of A red (JA
red) is another critical parameter for evaluating the electrocatalytic performance of the CE, which presents a similar trend to that of PCE (Fig. 5b). Apparently, the CQDs-P/rGO-4 displays the largest JA
red among various CEs, demonstrating the best electrocatalytic ability. To further quantitatively interpret the origin of the catalytic activity, the electrochemically active surface area (S e) of samples was measured, and the S e values against the I D:I G values were plotted. In Fig. 5(c and d), the variation of S e value is positively correlated with the I D:I G value, implying that the enhancement of defect density is the key contributor to the improvement of catalytic activity.

The kinetics of interfacial chemical reaction and electrochemical stability of CEs were elucidated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Apparently, the fitted Nyquist plots of rGO and CQDs-P/rGO-x exhibit three semicircles (Fig. S20a), which coincide with a novel model for porous electrode materials [39]. The intercept on the real axis signifies the series resistance (R s) related to the affinity ability between CE and FTO glass. The middle semicircle represents the charge transfer resistance (R ct) at the electrolyte/catalyst interface, associated with the catalytic activity of the catalyst for IRR. For the Pt reference, the left semicircle stands for R ct. In Fig. S20(b), the R s and R ct values of CQDs-P/rGO-4 are significantly smaller than those of Pt, suggesting the reduced charge loss during interfacial transport and improved charge collection efficiency, thereby boosting the IRR. Additionally, electrochemical stability is also a significant indicator for evaluating the performance of CE in practical applications of DSSCs. The sequential impedance measurements (Fig. 5e) show the R ct variation of CQDs-P/rGO-4 and Pt versus the EIS scan number, in which the R ct values of CQDs-P/rGO-4 change slightly, while those of Pt increase significantly, denoting the superior electrochemical stability of CQDs-P/rGO-4. Meanwhile, the CQDs-P/rGO-4 electrode maintains its original 3D spatial-scale structure before and after sequential measurements, further illustrating its excellent stability (Fig. S21).

Tafel polarization curves were measured to further corroborate the excellent catalytic activity of CQDs-P/rGO-4 (Fig. S22). Conspicuously, CQDs-P/rGO-4 achieves the highest exchange current density (J 0) and limiting diffusion current density (J lim) in comparison to others, revealing the optimum catalytic activity and the fastest diffusion rate. The above series of electrochemical measurements confirm that CQDs-P with intrinsically pentagon-rich defects contribute significantly to the improvement of catalytic activity.

To well elucidate the intrinsic contribution of pentagon defects to the activity of IRR, DFT calculations were carried out. The overall IRR occurring on the CE can be described as I3 − + 2e−
→
3I−, including a rapidly equilibrating non-electrochemical reaction in the electrolyte (I3 −
→
I2 + I−) and a two-electron transfer electrochemical reaction at surface/interface of catalyst (I2 + 2e−
→
2I−), which is considered to be the rate-determining step of the overall reaction [40–42]. The adsorption of the reaction intermediate I2 molecule on the catalyst surface is the initial and prerequisite step that impacts the charge transfer kinetics of the subsequent electrocatalytic reaction. For exploring the differences in the electron transfer and adsorption behavior of various configurations, the edge-hosted pentagon (C5-e) and basal plane-hosted pentagon adsorption structure models (C5-b) were adopted and optimized (Fig. S23). The charge density difference and Bader charge analysis for the optimized configurations are depicted in Fig. 6 (a). It is obvious that the charge transfers from the all-carbon-based catalyst to the I2 molecule, thereby facilitating the reduction of I2. The number of charge transferred from pentagon sites over C5-e and C5-b sites to I2 is evaluated to be 0.143 and 0.153 e, respectively. However, the number of transferred charge at the edge-hosted hexagon sites (C6-e-C5-e and C6-e-C5-b) or basal plane-hosted hexagon sites (C6-b-C5-e and C6-b-C5-b) over the above two structure models (0.031–0.096 e) is less than that of pentagon sites, further indicative of the electron-donating ability of pentagon sites is greater than that of hexagon ones, which benefits the reduction reaction.

Subsequently, the adsorption behaviors were quantitatively analyzed by adsorption energy. The adsorption energies of I2 on the edge-hosted pentagon sites (−1.64 eV) and basal plane-hosted pentagon sites (−0.42 eV) are larger than that on hexagon sites (Fig. 6b), corroborating that the adsorption sites with pentagon defects favor the adsorption of I2 for overall reduction from the thermodynamic perspective. Correspondingly, the distances of I–C (Distance of adsorbed I2 molecule to carbon adsorption sites) at pentagon sites are shorter than others (Fig. 6b), which also reveals that the pentagon sites exhibit a stronger adsorption ability for I2. Owing to the strong interactions, the bond length of I–I on the pentagon/hexagon sites (Fig. 6c) increases by 0.02–0.54 Å compared with that of the free I2 molecule (2.68 Å). Especially, I–I bond is elongated to 3.22 Å on the edge-hosted pentagon sites, indicating that the pentagon sites have a better activation/dissociation ability for the I2 molecule. The above theoretical calculations uncover that intrinsic pentagon defects in all-carbon-based catalysts promote electron transfer and modulate the adsorption/dissociation behavior of reaction intermediates, being the main catalytic active sites for IRR (Fig. 6d). As a consequence, the rational construction of carbon-based catalysts by intrinsic defect engineering is also an effective strategy to enhance their catalytic activity.

In conclusion, the work function and the p-band center calculations elucidate that intrinsic pentagon defects in carbon matrix exhibit excellent electrochemical activity owing to the favorable electrons overflow and the promoted binding affinity towards the intermediates of catalytic reaction, thus they can serve as the potential active sites in electrocatalytic reactions. In view of the positive effect of intrinsic pentagon defects in carbon materials, we adopted a C60 cage-opening strategy to elaborately construct the specific pentagon defects-structured carbon quantum dots (CQDs-P) made of carbon fragments, which was confirmed by LDI-TOF-MS and Raman spectra. The CQDs-P feature an abundance of inherited topological pentagon defects that increase the density of potentially catalytic active sites. Such enriched defect sites can effectively increase the micro-scale defect density of rGO via π-π interactions and further enhance the electrocatalytic activity of CQDs-P/rGO on the macroscopic scale. Utilizing IRR as a demo, the multiple spatial-scale CQDs-P/rGO-4 catalyst with high intrinsic activity and fast electron-transfer capability exhibits excellent I3 − reduction activity with a maximum PCE of 8.40%, superior to that of commercial Pt (7.97%). Theoretical calculations clarify the intrinsic contribution of specific pentagon sites to the catalytic activity of CQDs-P/rGO. The results indicate that the topological pentagon sites facilitate the charge transfer and modulate the adsorption/dissociation behavior, thereby significantly improving the photovoltaic performance. This research fundamentally elucidates the relationships between specific intrinsic defects and catalytic activity, providing atomic design principles for developing efficient carbon-based electrocatalysts.
Experimental details can be found in the Supporting Information.

Siyi Hou: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xuedan Song: Software, Visualization. Chang Yu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision. Jiangwei Chang: Visualization. Yiwang Ding: Visualization. Yingbin Liu: Visualization. Xiubo Zhang: Visualization. Weizhe Liu: Visualization. Jieshan Qiu: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.
